comments.text: I got GPT-5.4 Pro to do a search through literature to find the right statement. There are 2 variants possible, both solved. Here's a note on it.
Thanks. I ran standard check which claimed 1 minor math issue and objected to some historical presentation in there.

From the examples of $f(t)$, it seems that this counts the number of points on a triangular lattice within a ball of radius $t$ centered on some point in the lattice. Perhaps the original question is asking whether the triangular lattice is the densest way to pack points in $\mathbb{R}^2$ under the restriction that the pairwise distance between points is at least 1.

Even then the given values of $f(t)$ aren't quite right; $f(1)=6$ and $f(\sqrt3)=12$ are fine, but the next few should be $f(2)=18$, $f(\sqrt7)=30$ and $f(3)=36$. In any case, I do agree that the idea behind the question was likely to ask about the densest possible packing of points with minimal distance 1 between any pair of them. However, this is equivalent to asking about the densest packing of diameter 1 circles, but László Fejes Tóth proved already in 1942 the optimality of the hexagonal packing (which puts the centers of the circles on a triagonal lattice) so it didn't really make much sense to ask about it half a century later.
